case_name: Simon Dockswell v. Bethesda Memorial Hospital, Inc., etc.

summary.brief: Simon Dockswell v. Bethesda Memorial Hospital, Inc., etc.. Medical malpractice case involving oncology.

evidence_index.text: On April 29, 2011, Simon Dockswell was admitted to Respondent’s Bethesda Memorial Hospital for a 'colon resection due to cancerous polyps.'
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: A nurse removed the drainage tube. Evidence identifies Nurse Porges as the nurse who removed the drainage tube.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Mr. Dockswell experienced no immediate discomfort, but a 4.25-inch section of the tube was unknowingly left inside him.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The foreign-body presumption of negligence set forth in section 766.102(3)(b) is mandatory when a foreign body is found inside the patient’s body.
evidence_index.speaker: Court conclusion

evidence_index.text: The trial court ultimately denied the Dockswells’ request for Instruction 402.4c, reasoning that the Dockswells’ ability to identify Nurse Porges as the person who removed the drainage tube rendered the Instruction inapplicable.
evidence_index.speaker: Trial court reasoning

evidence_index.text: The jury returned a verdict for Bethesda Memorial Hospital, and the trial court entered judgment consistent with the verdict.
evidence_index.speaker: Trial court conclusion

evidence_index.text: We conclude that the presence of the drainage tube fragment in Mr. Dockswell’s body following discharge from Bethesda Memorial Hospital entitled the Dockswells to the benefit of the foreign-body presumption.
evidence_index.speaker: Court reasoning

evidence_index.text: The trial court’s failure to properly place the burden on Bethesda Memorial Hospital to disprove negligence and the verdict at trial in the hospital’s favor, we conclude that harmful error occurred.
evidence_index.speaker: Court reasoning

evidence_index.text: Accordingly, we approve of the Third District’s decision in Kenyon v. Miller, 756 So.2d 133 (Fla. 3d DCA 2000), quash the Fourth District’s decision in Dockswell, and remand with instructions that a new trial be ordered.
evidence_index.speaker: Court conclusion

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Admitted for colon resection due to cancerous polyps. Postoperative care involved insertion of a drainage tube.

requestables.name: Postoperative CT scan
requestables.description: CT scan ordered after patient complained of lingering abdominal pain

reveal.result_summary: 4.25-inch section of drainage tube left inside
reveal.decision_impact: Should prompt consideration of foreign-body presumption of negligence

timeline_phases.name: Hospital Admission
timeline_phases.description: Patient admitted for colon resection due to cancerous polyps

timeline_phases.name: Postoperative Care
timeline_phases.description: Postoperative management including drainage tube insertion and removal

key_events.event: Drainage tube removed by Nurse Porges

timeline_phases.name: Follow-up and Discovery
timeline_phases.description: Discovery of retained foreign body during follow-up

key_events.event: CT scan reveals retained drainage tube fragment

decision_points.prompt: What should be done to ensure the drainage tube is completely removed?

options.description: Ensure complete removal of the drainage tube with verification
options.clinical_reasoning: Standard practice to verify complete removal of medical devices
Failed to ensure complete removal, leading to retained foreign body

actual_action_defendant.description: Nurse removed drainage tube without verification

expected_action_court.description: Complete removal of drainage tube should have been verified
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Standard practice to prevent retained foreign bodies

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to verify complete removal led to retained foreign body
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Verification of complete removal should have been performed

patient_outcome.description: Retained drainage tube fragment discovered post-discharge

key_findings: Jury found no negligence on the part of Bethesda Memorial Hospital
Trial court's failure to instruct on foreign-body presumption was harmful error

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to verify complete removal of drainage tube
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Verification would have prevented retained foreign body

factual_timeline.event: Drainage tube removed by Nurse Porges
CT scan reveals retained drainage tube fragment

tests_performed.result: 4.25-inch section of drainage tube left inside

allegations.allegation: Failure to verify complete removal of drainage tube
